front: Poem: opens 'Razors pain you; / Rivers are damp' and catalogs downsides of various suicide methods including 'Acids stain you,' 'Drugs cause cramp,' 'Gas smells awful'; concludes 'You might as well live'
Poem: entire text reads "Men seldom make passes / At girls who wear glasses"
Short Story: protagonist Hazel Morse attempts suicide with twenty tablets of veronal but fails
Short Story: wakes to share a drink with maid Nettie
Short Story: protagonist toasts "Here's mud in your eye" and is called a "good sport" by men
Short Story: her husband Herbie leaves for Detroit
Short Story: protagonist's behavior at the theater is a "standing joke" because she cries at everything including "tiny garments" and "love both unrequited and mutual"
Poem: opens "Woman wants monogamy; Man delights in novelty" — asks "What earthly good can come of it?" after noting "Count to ten, and man is bored"
back: General Review of the Sex Situation (Dorothy Parker)
work: General Review of the Sex Situation

front: Poem: three stanzas extolling a lover's virtues, each ending with lines like "I wish I'd never met him" or "I wish somebody'd shoot him"
Poem: catalogs "Love, curiosity, freckles, and doubt" as "four of the things I'd rather be without" and lists "envy, content, and sufficient champagne" as unattainable
Poem: speaker asks "Why is it no one ever sent me yet / One perfect limousine, do you suppose?"
Short Story: two stenographers Annabel and Midge play "what-would-you-do-if-you-had-a-million-dollars" and argue over silver-fox coats
Short Story: consists entirely of a woman's thoughts as she waits for a man who called her "darling" twice to telephone
Author: wrote "Constant Reader" book reviews for The New Yorker
Author: savaged A.A. Milne with the line "Tonstant Weader Fwowed up" over use of the word "hummy"
Author: got her start replacing P.G. Wodehouse at Vanity Fair
Author: co-wrote the screenplay for A Star is Born with husband Alan Campbell
Author: parodied Housman, imagining "how gay 'twould be / to hang me from a flowering tree"
Author: wrote a response to Marlowe's "Passionate Shepherd" with the line "where a Freud in need is a Freud indeed / we'll always be Jung together"
Author: short story "Lolita" about jealous mother Mrs. Ewing was published three weeks before Nabokov's novel of the same name
Author: fictionalized as Gora Dwight, "disillusioned in regard to men," in Gertrude Atherton's novel Black Oxen
